indicator_text: Lobbying regulation in the UK is among the very weakest of countries that have developed lobbying disclosure laws.
source_quote: Lobbying regulation in the UK is among the very weakest of countries that have developed lobbying disclosure laws [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:73-74]
notes: One of five numbered "aspects" the authors say "together suggest that the Government purposely chose to keep lobbying in the UK in the dark" (lines 69-71). Not a scored item — a thesis bullet. Substantiated by re-applying CPI's Hired Guns rubric to the UK (separate sub-claims captured below as cpi.* corrections).

indicator_text: The set of lobby clients listed in the register is significantly different from the set of groups with whom ministerial offices report having met.
source_quote: The set of lobby clients listed in the register is significantly different from the set of groups with whom ministerial offices report having met [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:75-76]
notes: Quantitative finding (44% / 29% / 4% / 2% overlap rates, lines 467-468 and 533-537).

indicator_text: Collating information about ministerial meetings is too burdensome for any layperson to do.
source_quote: Collating information about ministerial meetings is too burdensome for any layperson to do [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:77-78]
notes: Operationalised through the seven usability criteria (usability.* below). 1045 files in 7 different formats across 23 departments (lines 322-324, 442-444).

indicator_text: The debate surrounding lobbying transparency as the law was being written made clear that the proposal was not robust enough.
source_quote: The debate surrounding lobbying transparency as the law was being written made clear that the proposal was not robust enough [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:79-80]
notes: Process-tracing claim — 85% of 259 consultation comments wanted stricter rules (Crepaz 2017a, lines 271-272); Public Administration Select Committee 2009 had recommended much more stringent regulations (lines 241-247).

indicator_text: Lobbying clients meet more frequently with Government ministers than unregistered groups, and a small number of lobby clients dominate over the others.
source_quote: Lobbying clients meet more frequently with Government ministers than unregistered groups, and a small number of lobby clients dominate over the others [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:81-82]
notes: Empirical: top 10% of groups account for 60% of meetings; top 1% account for 27% (lines 591-594). Average lobby client meets ministers 17 times vs 3-4 times for the average meeting-data group (lines 560-562).

indicator_text: Accuracy (preciseness, factuality) of disclosed transparency data — does the disclosure offer policy-specific information rather than vague filler labels, and are organizational affiliations identified?
source_quote: Accuracy (preciseness, factuality) The meetings data offer little information about the meetings other than the broad 'purpose of the meeting.' Among the most commonly stated purposes are generic descriptions such as 'trade and investment,' 'energy issues,' or 'tax matters.' … A total of 10% of our acquired reports fail to offer any policy-specific information about what was discussed in any of the meetings listed. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:341-350]
notes: Adopted from Piotrowski & Liao (2012). UK assessment: poor — vague purposes, ~10% of reports give no policy-specific info; 5.2% of meeting-data entries identify a person without organizational affiliation (line 364). Authors invoke Piotrowski & Liao's "intentional concealment" label (line 372).

indicator_text: Accessibility of disclosed transparency data — is online access public and open, with no registration required, and are files downloadable?
source_quote: Accessibility Online access to the meetings data is public and open; no registration is necessary. All files can be accessed via either the general gov.uk website or the publications section of the individual department websites. All files can be downloaded in the format provided by the source. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:374-378]
notes: Adopted from Piotrowski & Liao (2012). UK assessment: positive — files are publicly downloadable; .csv files can be previewed in-browser.

indicator_text: Completeness of disclosed transparency data — are all required reports filed, and do the data cover all relevant levels of officials and all departments?
source_quote: Completeness By the middle of 2016, 92.6% of the 877 required quarterly reports were available for the years 2011–2015. … However, the 5000 senior civil servants at the next levels do not have to report their meetings, nor do Special Advisers to ministers (their political staff). One department, Export Finance, had not published any meeting disclosures by mid-2016. The incomplete data provided and nondisclosure of meetings with other levels of government suggest a substantial shortfall in fulfilling Piotrowski and Liao's (2012) specification that 'all necessary parts' be published. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:380-393]
notes: Adopted from Piotrowski & Liao (2012). UK assessment: poor — 877 expected reports = 20 quarters × 44 department positions, minus 3 for the abolished Deputy PM office; 92.6% filing rate; coverage gaps for civil servants, Special Advisers, junior ministers, and one whole department (Export Finance).

indicator_text: Understandability of disclosed transparency data — are reports written in non-technical language, avoiding uncommon acronyms?
source_quote: Understandability All meetings disclosures are arranged and written in a way that makes them easily understandable. For the most part, the reports eschew technical language and the use of uncommon acronyms. Filers could possibly argue that the use of generic statements of the meetings' purposes increases their understandability for the lay public; however, their lack of accuracy and completeness is better described as a hindrance to transparency and open government. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:395-400]
notes: Adopted from Piotrowski & Liao (2012). UK assessment: surface-positive but the authors note this trades off with accuracy.

indicator_text: Timeliness of disclosed transparency data — are quarterly reports uploaded promptly after the close of the relevant quarter?
source_quote: Timeliness As mentioned above, our data collection during the third and fourth quarters of 2016 revealed that 7.4% of quarterly reports from the years 2011 to 2015 were still not available online. Moreover, almost 30% of reports about the first quarter of 2016 and 21% of reports about the fourth quarter of 2015 had not yet been uploaded, which indicates a failure on the part of departments to provide their transparency information in a timely manner. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:402-410]
notes: Adopted from Piotrowski & Liao (2012). UK assessment: poor — late filings widespread.

indicator_text: Free or low cost of access to disclosed transparency data — apart from acquisition costs and internet access fees, is the information free?
source_quote: Free or low cost Apart from acquisition costs and internet access fees, access to the quarterly meetings reports is free of charge. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:412-413]
notes: Adopted from Piotrowski & Liao (2012). UK assessment: positive.

indicator_text: Searchability of disclosed transparency data — does a complete, dedicated searchable database exist that supports comprehensive, comparative, and targeted queries?
source_quote: Following Holman and Luneburg (2012), we add as another criterion whether the provided data are searchable, so that comprehensive, comparative, and specially targeted database queries are possible. … Searchability The biggest usability obstacle is the lack of a complete and searchable database of ministerial meetings with external groups. Meeting reports are found in the publications database at gov.uk; they do not have their own site. … It is also not possible to do a reliable targeted search for either external groups or Ministers/Secretaries. Considerable time and skill are required to collect, synthesize, and clean the disparate data. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:336-338, 415-427]
notes: The one criterion the authors *add* to Piotrowski & Liao's set (sourced from Holman & Luneburg 2012). UK assessment: poor — no dedicated meetings site, no targeted search possible. Authors classify the UK in Piotrowski & Liao's "overload" quadrant — disclosed in large amounts but with insufficient attention to information usability (lines 430-434).

indicator_text: Re-coding of CPI's "minimum threshold for money spent" item — UK lobby firms need not register if they do not have a VAT number (annual revenue ≥ £85,000); CPI gives 0 points if the threshold is higher than $500.
section_or_category: Re-application of CPI Hired Guns rubric to UK
scoring_rule: 0 points (threshold > $500). Authors revise from Keeling et al. (2017)'s 4 points down to 0.
source_quote: Keeling et al. gave the UK full credit (4 points under CPI's scheme) for requiring every lobbyist to register with no minimum threshold for money spent. In fact, however, UK lobby firms need not register if they do not have a VAT number, which requires an annual revenue of £85,000 or more. The CPI gives 0 points if the threshold is higher than $500. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:194-199]
notes: NOT an item McKay & Wozniak created — they re-score one CPI Hired Guns line for the UK. Captured because their explicit re-coding produces the paper's headline regulatory-weakness conclusion. Combined effect with cpi.correction.individual_lobbyists below: total UK score reduced from 33 to 26, moving the UK from "medium robustness" to "low robustness" (lines 203-205).

indicator_text: Re-coding of CPI's "individual lobbyists must register" item — only consultant lobbying firms must register, not the individuals who work for them, nor in-house lobbyists; CPI gives 0 points if individual lobbyists are not required to register.
section_or_category: Re-application of CPI Hired Guns rubric to UK
scoring_rule: 0 points (individual lobbyists not required to register). Authors revise from Keeling et al. (2017)'s 3 points down to 0.
source_quote: Keeling et al. give the UK credit (3 points) for requiring individual lobbyists to register, when in fact only consultant lobbying firms must register, not the individuals who work for the consultancy, nor any in-house lobbyists. CPI gives 0 points if individual lobbyists are not required to register. These corrections reduce the UK total score from 33 to 26, and this score moves the UK into the 'low robustness' category [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:199-205]
notes: Second of two CPI corrections by the authors. See cpi.correction.threshold_for_registration note.

indicator_text: The 5-year-old lobby register misses the majority of lobbying that occurs in London, especially lobbying by those firms and campaigning organizations that lobby on their own behalf; names, targets, and expenditures of lobbyists are not reported, no subject matter being discussed, and no limitations on gift-giving or revolving-door employment.
source_quote: First, the 5-year-old lobby register misses the majority of lobbying that occurs in London, especially lobbying by those firms and campaigning organizations that lobby on their own behalf. The names, targets, and expenditures of lobbyists are not reported, let alone the subject matter being discussed, and there are no limitations on gift-giving or revolving-door employment. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:695-700]
notes: Conclusion-section restatement; substantively overlaps with thesis.aspect_1 above. Captured separately because the conclusion enumerates four critiques (the introduction had five aspects), and the four implicitly bundle aspects 4 and 5 into critique 4 below.

indicator_text: The Government's published data about ministers' meetings with outside groups, touted as transparency, are almost totally unusable as provided; applying Piotrowski & Liao (2012) plus Holman & Luneburg (2012)'s searchability criterion, the usability of meetings data is low.
source_quote: Second, the Government's published data about ministers' meetings with outside groups, which were touted as an effort toward transparency, are almost totally unusable as provided. Applying the criteria for the usability of government information laid out by Piotrowski and Liao (2012), with the addition of Holman and Luneburg's (2012) searchability criterion, we conclude that the usability of the meetings data is low. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:703-707]
notes: Restatement of thesis.aspect_3 / the seven usability.* items.

indicator_text: Very little overlap between lobby register and ministerial meetings data — lobby register contains <4% of organizations known to have met with government ministries; meetings data contain no records for as many as 70% of clients known to have contracted with professional lobbying firms.
source_quote: Third, there is very little overlap between the two sets of data. Our analysis of more than 72,000 meetings reported by government departments shows that the lobby register contains less than 4% of the organizations that we know met with government ministries. Conversely, data about ministerial meetings data contain no records of meetings with as many as 70% of the clients we know contracted with professional lobbying firms. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:708-716]

indicator_text: Tremendous skew in the distribution of ministers' meetings — majority of meetings held with just 10% of groups; more than a quarter of meetings with just 1% of groups.
source_quote: Fourth, we note a tremendous skew in the distribution of ministers' meetings with lobby groups. The majority of meetings are held with just 10% of the groups, and more than a quarter of meetings are with just 1% of groups. This skew may be the kind of private information that the Government does not wish to make public. [papers/text/McKay_Wozniak_2020__lobbying_transparency_uk.txt:717-720]
